findings: Identity, common and scientific names, and family are correct for Palm Warbler (Setophaga palmarum; Parulidae).
The illustrated bird has one head, one pointed bill, two legs with plausible passerine feet, a single tail, and two wings (one displayed in profile); no duplicated or missing anatomy is visible.

Plumage is a credible Palm Warbler, especially a breeding Western-type/intergrade presentation: chestnut cap, pale eyebrow and dark eye line, brownish-olive upperparts, yellow throat/undertail area, pale streaked belly, long legs and tail. The bill is slightly heavy-looking for the species but remains acceptably slender and pointed.

All visible plate text is substantively supported by the sources, including size, habitat, tail-wagging/ground-foraging behavior, and field marks. Cornell gives the plate’s stated 7–13 g weight range; Audubon instead gives 8–13 g, a minor source discrepancy rather than a plate error.
The plate contains no place name, ZIP code, coordinates, map, or local-observation detail.

verification_sources.title: Palm Warbler Identification — All About Birds
